title: Hong Kong MPF Research — Fund Scheme Performance, Sales, and Retention
situation: Hong Kong's MPF (Mandatory Provident Fund) market is highly competitive and fund scheme performance is a visible driver of plan sponsor and member behavior. Manulife's Asia retirement business needed a clearer empirical view of how fund performance was actually translating into sales and retention outcomes in the HK market, and whether the existing approach to selecting and retaining external asset managers was aligned with the commercial reality.
obstacle: The relationship between fund performance and commercial outcomes in MPF is widely assumed but rarely rigorously tested. Building the analysis required integrating performance data with sales and retention data, controlling for plan-level variables, and translating findings into something executives could act on without overinterpreting noisy data.
actions: Designed and led an original research project examining the relationship between fund scheme performance and sales and retention outcomes in the Hong Kong MPF market
Integrated performance, sales, and retention data and applied analytical controls to test the strength of the relationship
Synthesized findings into clear strategic implications for asset manager selection and retention
Presented insights to senior leaders, supporting decisions about which external asset managers to hire, retain, or replace
Combined the findings with broader competitive intelligence on the HK MPF landscape
results: Research findings directly influenced asset manager hiring and retention decisions in the Hong Kong business
Provided a more rigorous evidence base for commercial decisions previously driven largely by intuition
Strengthened the analytical credibility of the Asia insights function with regional executives
fullStory: The premise was simple but rarely tested with rigor: in Hong Kong's MPF market, does fund performance actually translate into sales and retention, and if so, how strongly? I designed the research, integrated the data, and tested the relationships. The findings were specific enough to act on, and they did inform real decisions about which external asset managers to hire and retain. Beyond the conclusions, the work demonstrated what competitive intelligence looks like when it is grounded in your own data rather than borrowed from industry reports.
